APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work addresses joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing, a core NLP problem appropriate for top NLP/ML venues, and targets the right audience (parsing/SRL and neural methods). The formulation (transition-based joint parser with stack LSTMs) and evaluation (CoNLL 2008/2009 benchmarks, F1 metrics, pretrained embeddings) follow community standards. Empirical claims of superior performance, linear-time greedy inference, released code, and reported hyperparameters make it well positioned and reproducible for a top conference. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing (semantic role labeling) using modern neural methods (stack LSTMs, end-to-end training) and evaluates on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2008/2009) with F1 metrics. The methodology (greedy transition-based parser with linear-time inference, pretrained embeddings, reported hyperparameters, and available code) and the reported baselines/benchmarks align with expectations for conference submissions. Related work coverage is adequate and the topic clearly targets the NLP/ML research community concerned with parsing and structured prediction.

The methodology (greedy transition-based parser with linear-time inference, pretrained embeddings, reported hyperparameters, and available code) and the reported baselines/benchmarks align with expectations for conference submissions. SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear technical claims (a joint transition-based parser using stack LSTMs, greedy linear-time inference, end-to-end training) and provides empirical results on CoNLL 2008/2009, but the summary does not report which baselines or statistical tests were used to support the claim of superior performance, weakening the experimental evidence. No mathematical derivations are reported in the summary, and an acknowledged limitation (1.5% of sentences cannot be encoded by the transition sequence) plus the heuristic of defaulting unseen predicates to sense “01” are concrete issues that could affect evaluation. Overall the approach appears technically plausible, but missing experimental controls/baselines and the encoding/sense-disambiguation caveats justify moderate concern about the strength of the empirical support. | B(4): The main claims (a greedy, transition-based joint syntactic+semantic parser using stack LSTMs, trained end-to-end, achieving superior results on CoNLL 2008/2009) are clearly stated and appear supported by experimental results on those datasets. The model formulation (four stack LSTMs summarizing parser state and a classifier selecting transitions) and training procedure are technically sound. However, the experimental methodology is under-specified in the summary (no baselines explicitly mentioned and no mention of statistical significance testing), which weakens empirical support. The paper acknowledges specific limitations (1.5% of sentences not encodable, defaulting unseen predicates to sense “01”), so conclusions are largely justified but not flawless.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear technical claims (a joint transition-based parser using stack LSTMs, greedy linear-time inference, end-to-end training) and provides empirical results on CoNLL 2008/2009, but the summary does not report which baselines or statistical tests were used to support the claim of superior performance, weakening the experimental evidence. No mathematical derivations are reported in the summary, and an acknowledged limitation (1.5% of sentences cannot be encoded by the transition sequence) plus the heuristic of defaulting unseen predicates to sense “01” are concrete issues that could affect evaluation. Overall the approach appears technically plausible, but missing experimental controls/baselines and the encoding/sense-disambiguation caveats justify moderate concern about the strength of the empirical support.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims (a greedy, transition-based joint syntactic+semantic parser using stack LSTMs, trained end-to-end, achieving superior results on CoNLL 2008/2009) are clearly stated and appear supported by experimental results on those datasets. The model formulation (four stack LSTMs summarizing parser state and a classifier selecting transitions) and training procedure are technically sound. However, the experimental methodology is under-specified in the summary (no baselines explicitly mentioned and no mention of statistical significance testing), which weakens empirical support. The paper acknowledges specific limitations (1.5% of sentences not encodable, defaulting unseen predicates to sense “01”), so conclusions are largely justified but not flawless.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper applies stack LSTMs to encode a finite-length representation of the entire joint parser state (four stack LSTMs for syntactic stack, semantic stack, buffer, and action history) and uses this in a greedy, transition-based algorithm that jointly produces syntactic trees and directed SRL graphs. Joint syntactic+semantic dependency parsing is a well-studied problem, but using end-to-end learned stack-LSTM state summaries to drive linear-time greedy joint parsing without expert-crafted features is a non-trivial and clearly differentiated technical contribution. The approach therefore represents notable novelty rather than a purely incremental reuse of existing methods. | B(4): The paper applies and extends stack LSTMs to encode the entire joint parser state (four stacks: syntactic, semantic, buffer, action history) and defines a transition-based, greedy joint syntactic+semantic dependency parser trained end-to-end — a clear methodological advance over prior joint models. The problem (joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing) is well-studied, but the approach (finite-length vector state via stack LSTMs, linear-time greedy inference without handcrafted full-parse features) is a non-trivial and well-differentiated contribution. This would be of clear interest to experts, though it builds on existing neural parsing architectures rather than proposing an entirely new problem or paradigm.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing and reports superior performance on the CoNLL 2008 and 2009 English shared tasks, indicating practical value. Its approach — learning a finite-length joint parser state with stack LSTMs, end-to-end training, and linear-time greedy inference without expert-crafted full-parse features — can shift how researchers design efficient joint parsers. Code availability and efficiency make the method likely to be adopted and extended, though small limitations (≈1.5% encoding errors and defaulting unseen predicate senses) somewhat temper its reach. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing and demonstrates clear empirical gains, reporting superior performance on the CoNLL 2008 and 2009 English shared tasks. Its use of stack LSTMs to learn a finite vector summary of the entire joint parser state and a linear-time greedy inference algorithm (including feature extraction) removes reliance on expert-crafted full-parse features, which is likely to change engineering trade-offs and inspire follow-up neural transition-based joint parsers. The end-to-end training, public code, and efficiency make the approach likely to be widely used and built upon, opening productive research directions in efficient joint parsing. While not necessarily transformative across all of NLP, it is a strong-impact contribution within parsing and semantic role labeling.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper reports competitive results on CoNLL 2008/2009 and gives implementation details (stack LSTMs, training, hyperparameters) with code available, supporting reproducibility. However, the summary indicates no explicit baselines were mentioned and ablation/sensitivity analyses are not reported, leaving important questions about what components drive performance. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations (encoding errors, default sense for unseen predicates) are acknowledged. Overall the experiments are adequate but missing key analyses and comparative baselines. | B(3): The work reports superior performance on CoNLL 2008 and 2009 and provides code and hyperparameter details, which support reproducibility. However, the summary notes no explicit baselines were mentioned and does not indicate any ablation or sensitivity analyses of the model components (e.g., the four stack LSTMs or classifier), which are important for attributing gains. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations (encoding errors for 1.5% of sentences and unseen-predicate sense defaults) are discussed. Overall the experiments are adequate to demonstrate effectiveness but miss important comparative and ablation analyses.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The summary indicates no explicit baselines are reported (“Baselines compared: none mentioned”), even though standard datasets (CoNLL 2008/2009) and F1 are used and the paper claims superior performance over prior joint models. Because no detailed baseline comparisons are documented, I cannot judge fairness of comparisons or whether the paper acknowledges when prior methods are comparable/better, nor whether differences are analyzed. The paper does appear to cite related work adequately, but overall comparisons to prior work are inadequate and key baseline details are missing. | B(2): The provided summary does not list any specific baselines compared ("Baselines compared: none mentioned"), even though the paper claims superior performance to previous joint models on CoNLL 2008/2009. While the paper uses standard datasets (CoNLL 2008/2009) and F1, the absence of explicit baseline details or acknowledgement of when prior methods perform comparably prevents judging fairness or thoroughness. Related work coverage is noted as adequate, but that alone does not substitute for concrete, fair empirical comparisons, so the comparisons are inadequate.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized (intro → methodology describing four stack LSTMs and a classifier → datasets and experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (a greedy transition-based parser that jointly produces syntactic trees and directed SRL graphs). Notation seems consistent and well-defined in the summary (finite-length vector representations, stack LSTMs, allowed transitions and classifier are clearly described), and writing quality is noted as good with adequate related-work coverage and reported hyperparameters. The summary does not specify the quality of figures/tables/examples, so presentation there is unclear, which prevents a top score. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized and presents its contributions early (an incremental greedy transition-based joint parser using stack LSTMs, a classifier for transitions, and linear-time greedy inference with strong CoNLL 2008/2009 results). The methodology and datasets are clearly described (four stack LSTMs, end-to-end training, datasets and pretrained embeddings), and writing quality is reported as good with adequate related-work coverage. REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper reports hyperparameters and training details (end-to-end backprop, SGD, classifier architecture) and uses public datasets (CoNLL 2008/2009) and common pretrained embeddings, and the authors provide code—so reimplementation or exact replication should be largely possible. Evaluation is clearly defined (F1). However, the summary does not confirm a fully detailed data-preprocessing pipeline or every low-level implementation choice (e.g., exact handling of unseen predicates beyond the stated default), so a few minor details may require consulting the released code or contacting authors. | B(4): The paper provides model architecture and training details (four stack LSTMs, classifier, end-to-end backprop with SGD) and reports hyperparameters; code is available and the used datasets (CoNLL 2008/2009, Gigaword embeddings) are public, so most implementation details can be reproduced. However the summary does not clearly describe the data preprocessing pipeline and a few operational choices (e.g., how gold joint parses that cannot be encoded are handled, and the defaulting of unseen predicate senses) suggest minor implementation-level details may be missing. Overall, these are small gaps that likely do not prevent reproduction given the available code and reported hyperparameters.

main_claims: An incremental, greedy transition-based parser is presented that jointly produces syntactic dependency trees and directed SRL graphs.
The model learns a finite-length vector representation of the entire joint parser state using stack LSTMs and uses a classifier to select transitions.
The parser achieves superior performance compared to previous joint models on the CoNLL 2008 and 2009 English shared tasks.
The greedy inference algorithm runs in linear time (including feature extraction) and does not rely on expert-crafted full-parse features.
_summary.methodology: A transition-based joint syntactic and semantic dependency parser using four stack LSTMs (for syntactic stack, semantic stack, buffer, and action history) to encode parser state; a classifier (linear layer+ReLU) chooses allowed transitions based on the state summary, and the model is trained end-to-end with backprop and SGD.
limitations: For 1.5% of English sentences in the CoNLL 2009 dataset, the transition sequence incorrectly encodes the gold-standard joint parse.

At test time unseen predicates default to the “01” sense (which is correct 91.22% of predicates by type in the CoNLL 2009 English training data), so sense disambiguation for unseen predicates can be incorrect.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - joint syntactic and semantic dependency parsing (semantic role labeling)
